coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode luminescence and anti-counterfeiting application of YVO4: Eu3+@SiO2@CDs nanocomposites
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lü, Jiantao
Fan, Ting
Liang, Jie
Zhou, Yayun
Deng, Tingting
Jiang, Ruming
coredata.dc:description: With the combination of carbon quantum dots (CDs) and rare earth-doped fluorescent materials, multi-mode luminescence can be achieved in fluorescent ink, thereby enhancing its anti-counterfeiting security coefficient. However, the self-quenching process in CDs currently hinders their application in the field of anti-counterfeiting. In this study, we employed simple chemical method to prepare the SiO2-encapsulated YVO4:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites, which greatly improved the stability of the composite and achieved dual-mode solid-state luminescence. Quick response code anti-counterfeiting patterns were fabricated using screen printing technology, and dual-mode luminescence performance with red and blue emission was achieved under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm respectively. The YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2@CDs nanocomposites showed stable dual-mode luminescent performance in both solid-state and solvent environment, demonstrating great potential in anticounterfeiting and information protection application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Carbon quantum dots
Dual-mode luminescence
Anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of YVO4: Eu3+@SiO2@CDs nanocomposites.

(a-c) TEM images of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+, YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2 and YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs respectively; (d) HRTEM images of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs; (e) Typical X-ray diffraction patterns of the YVO4: X% Eu3+ (X=1, 3, 5, 8, 10), YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2 and YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs; (f) 2θ amplified XRD pattern of 23° ∼ 28°; (g-i) EDX of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+, YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2 and YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs respectively.
The XPS spectra of the YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs. (a) Overall spectrum, Core region spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) Si 2p and (e) N 1 s.
(a) FT-IR spectra of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+, YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2 and YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs; (b) Zeta potentials of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+, YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2 and YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs.

(a, b) Excitation spectra under λem=530 nm and Emission spectra excited by 365 nm of CDs; (c, d) Excitation spectra under λem=613 nm and Emission spectra excited by 254 nm of YVO4:X%Eu3+ (X=1,3,5,8,10).

(a) Excitation spectra of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs under λem=455 nm and λem=613 nm; (b) Emission spectra of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs excited by 254 nm and 365 nm (The insertions from the top to the bottom were photos of the composites excited by 254 nm and 365 nm light respectively); (c-e) The photographs of graphic security patterns made with YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs under daylight, 254 nm and 365 nm irradiation respectively from left to right.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode luminescence and anti-counterfeiting application of YVO4: Eu3+@SiO2@CDs nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Foshan University, Foshan 528200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Foshan University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Energy, Foshan University, Foshan 528000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Energy, Foshan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Energy, Foshan University, Foshan, 528000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Key Laboratory for Hydrogen Energy Technologies, Foshan 528000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Key Laboratory for Hydrogen Energy Technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Key Laboratory for Hydrogen Energy Technologies, Foshan, 528000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Foshan Inorganic Micro-nano Luminescent Materials Engineering Technology Research Center, Foshan 528000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Foshan Inorganic Micro-nano Luminescent Materials Engineering Technology Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Foshan inorganic micro-nano luminescent materials engineering technology research center, Foshan, 528000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Energy, Foshan University, Foshan 528000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Energy, Foshan University,

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the combination of carbon quantum dots (CDs) and rare earth-doped fluorescent materials, multi-mode luminescence can be achieved in fluorescent ink, thereby enhancing its anti-counterfeiting security coefficient. However, the self-quenching process in CDs currently hinders their application in the field of anti-counterfeiting. In this study, we employed simple chemical method to prepare the SiO2-encapsulated YVO4:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites, which greatly improved the stability of the composite and achieved dual-mode solid-state luminescence. Quick response code anti-counterfeiting patterns were fabricated using screen printing technology, and dual-mode luminescence performance with red and blue emission was achieved under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm respectively. The YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2@CDs nanocomposites showed stable dual-mode luminescent performance in both solid-state and solvent environment, demonstrating great potential in anticounterfeiting and information protection application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the rapidly developing information age, due to the counterfeit information will seriously affects social order with extremely negative consequences, information security is particularly important. As a simple anti-counterfeiting material, fluorescent ink has been widely used in information encoding, storage, and protection. Currently, the primary method for producing fluorescent ink is to composite two or more fluorescent materials to achieve multi-mode luminescence, thereby enhancing its anti-counterfeiting security coefficient, such as compositing carbon quantum dots (CDs) with rare earth-doped fluorescent materials. Rare earth-doped fluorescent materials have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent physicochemical properties, including low phonon energy, high chemical stability, and easily modifiable surface structure. These make them highly favoured in fields such as optoelectronic devices, fibre Lasers, and biomedical imaging. The luminescence of rare earth-doped fluorescent materials is essentially from the transition between f-f or f-d configurations of 4 f electrons. CDs are one of the highly regarded materials with outstanding characteristics such as high photoluminescence, chemical tunability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. They also demonstrate enormous potential in the field of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, etc.

However, almost all reports on CDs up to now have indicated that self-quenching phenomena occurs by suffering from excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) or π- π interactions similar in organic molecules. This restricts CDs from exhibiting good fluorescence performance only in dispersed solvents and prevents them from achieving solid-state luminescence, thus greatly hindering their development and application in the field of anti-counterfeiting [1–3]. A common method to avoid this adverse effect is to incorporate CDs into solid-state matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane, inorganic salts, or by employing heteroatom-doping methods to obtain carbon quantum dots capable of solid-state luminescence [4–6]. In practical applications, high-performance luminescent devices require a combination of the high photoluminescence quantum yields and high loading fraction of luminescent CDs centers.

Shen et al. have developed a green and simple hydrothermal method to carbonize boric acid and ethylenediamine into boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) [7]. The B-CDs have uniform morphology and abundant functional groups on their surface, enabling them to exhibit excellent fluorescence properties in both aqueous solution and solid state. The particularly unique solid-state fluorescence characteristics provide new insights for a wide range of applications, including LED converter materials and cell imaging agents. Liu et al. proposed an effective method to address the quenching issue of solid-state CDs [8]. They synthesized carbon quantum dot-hybridized silica nanospheres (CSNs) via a one-step hydrothermal method. The key step of this process involved mixing CDs with SOCl2 in acetonitrile solution at low temperature (50–60°C) to form acyl chloride-functionalized CDs under mild conditions. The acyl chloride-functionalized carbon quantum dots, serving as active intermediates, significantly enhanced the coupling efficiency between carboxyl groups and amine groups and causing carbon quantum dots to primarily aggregate within the silica matrix. This method prevents the self-quenching and external quenching. The fluorescence intensity of a single CSN can reach up to 3000 times that of a single carbon dot. Additionally, these CSNs have attracted promising applications in biological sensing and biomedicine due to their strong fluorescence stability, low toxicity, and abundant surface functional groups. Xu et al. demonstrated that carbon dots were embedded well in the silica layer coating on the surface of NaYF4: Yb,Tm nanoparticles, leading to the relatively stable structure of the nanocomposites [9]. The nanocomposites showed interesting dual-mode luminescent properties, i.e., blue green light under 365 nm UV lamp and blue purple light under 980 nm laser. The literature on rare earth nanocrystals, silica and carbon dot triple coating structure materials is very few. Simple UV light excited bimodal light-emitting anti-counterfeiting materials based on carbon dot core-shell structure need to be developed.

In this paper, for the first time, we optimized the fluorescent material of YVO4: Eu3+ nanocrystals combining with CDs by introducing a silica layer as intermediate medium. We used 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as amino silane coupling agents to enable binding of CDs to SiO2 nanoparticles via amide bonds [10–14]. The preparation process of YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2@CDs nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1. YVO4: Eu3+ nanoparticles were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. A layer of silica was coated on their surface, followed by compositing with CDs. CDs were attached to the surface of silica through siloxane groups and amino groups, forming a cross-linked structure [15]. The novel YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2@CDs composite system was innovatively developed by inhibiting the π-π interactions and excessive resonance energy transfer of block or powder form CDs, thereby improving the dispersibility of CDs and achieving solid-state luminescence after composite with YVO4:Eu3+. The prepared YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2@CDs emit red and blue fluorescence under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm respectively, demonstrating stable dual-mode luminescent performance in both solid-state and solvent environment. The QR code anti-counterfeiting patterns were prepared with the luminescent composite by screen printing technology, demonstrating great potential in anticounterfeiting.

Ln(NO3)3·6 H2O (Ln = Eu, Y), Na3VO4, citric acid, cetyltrimethylammonium ammonium bromide (CTAB), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Urea was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroform (CHCl3) and ammonia (NH3·H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

An appropriate amount of citric acid (3 g) and urea (6 g) were added to 10 mL of deionized water under stirring. The mixture was heated in household microwave oven for 5 minutes at 650 W. The resulting dark brown clusters was transferred to vacuum oven at 60°C for 1 hour. The obtained product was dissolved in deionized water and centrifuged to remove large particle impurities to obtain CDs aqueous solution.

An appropriate amount of Y(NO3)3, Eu(NO3)3 and Na3VO4 were dissolved in deionized water. NH3·H2O was added dropwise to the solution while adjusting the pH to 8–9. The mixture was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal kettle and kept at 140°C for 12 hours. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the obtained solid product was centrifuged and washed with anhydrous ethanol for three times, dried at 60°C for 6 hours.

The YVO4: 5 % Eu3+ (0.05 g) obtained from the previous step was dispersed in 5.6 mL of oleic acid. Then, 2 mL of chloroform was added under stirring. The mixture was sonicated for 2 minutes to achieve better dispersion. Subsequently, 0.02 mg CTAB was added, and the mixture was heated at 60°C for 1 hour. Then, 30 mL of water, 0.3 mL of NH3·H2O, 0.25 mL of TEOS, and 2.0 mL of ethyl acetate were added and stirring for 30 minutes. The resulting suspension was centrifuged with water and ethanol and dried in vacuum.

1000 mg YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2 was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol. Then, 130 μL of APTES was added to the solution and sonicate for 10 minutes, following by stirring for 5 hours. 10 mL of deionized water and 1 μL of CDs were added and stirred for 4 hours. The resulting suspension was centrifuged with water and ethanol and dried in vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of QR code anti-counterfeiting patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-curable adhesive was mixed with YVO4: Eu3+@SiO2@CDs composites and the mass ratio of powder-to-gel was approximately 10:1. After stirred evenly, the obtained patterns were printed using a customized 100-mesh screen. After being irradiated by UV lamp for 1 minute, the cured QR code anti-counterfeiting patterns were obtained.

The phase composition was recorded by Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Co., Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the samples was recorded by the iraffini-1 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Shimadzu, Co., Japan). The morphology and structure of the sample were characterized using the FEI Talos F200x transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 120 kV (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., United States). The Zeta potential spectrum of the sample was recorded using the Zeta potential and particle size analyzer from Brookhaven Instruments Corporation in the United States. The emission spectrum (PL) and excitation spectrum (PLE) of the sample were measured on the steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh, United Kingdom). The ZF-2 UV analyzer was used as the excitation light source for anti-counterfeiting patterns at 365 and 254 nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the sample was recorded using the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. The anti-counterfeiting pattern was photographed with Huawei P40.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of three samples (YVO4: 5 % Eu3+, YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2, YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs) were shown in Fig. 2(a-c). The YVO4 crystal synthesized by hydrothermal method exhibited a raisin-like shape with uniform size, while the short-axis ranged from 9 to 12 nm and long-axis ranged from 18 to 24 nm [16]. Fig. 2(b) illustrated that after coating with silica, the particle size significantly increased and became spherical with a size of around 170 nm. The spherical structure remained intact after the introduction of CDs. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope image (HRTEM) of the sample YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs with clear lattice fringes was shown in Fig. 2(d). The distance between two adjacent crystal planes was about 0.35 nm and 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (2 0 0) crystal plane of YVO4 and the (0 2 0) crystal plane of graphite carbon, respectively [16]. The result demonstrated the successful combination of YVO4 and CDs.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of YVO4: X% Eu3+ (X=1, 3, 5, 8, 10), YVO4:5 %Eu3+@SiO2, and YVO4:5 %Eu3+@SiO2@CDs were shown in Fig. 2(e). The diffraction peaks of YVO4 samples doped with different concentrations of Eu3+ matched with those of the standard card for tetragonal phase of YVO4 (JCPDS file 76–1649), and no other diffraction peaks appear, indicating that the doping of Eu3+ ions does not affect the crystal structure of YVO4 [17]. Additionally, the diffraction peaks of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2 after silica encapsulation showed no significant change. This could be attributed to two possible reasons: (1) Silica existed in an amorphous state, which does not affect the phase of YVO4. (2) The silica coating existed in the form of an amorphous hydrated oxide, with extremely weak diffraction intensity that was covered by the diffraction peaks of YVO4. The weak diffraction peaks around 2θ=23° in YVO4: 5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs after composite with CDs could be attributed to the amorphous nature of the CDs [17–20]. Fig. 2(f) showed that as the doping concentration of Eu3+ ions increase, the diffraction peaks of the samples gradually shifted to lower angles. This could be explained by Bragg's law λ=2dsinθ. As the Y3+ ions (CN=6, r=0.09 nm) gradually replaced by Eu3+ ions (CN=6, r=0.095 nm) in the crystal lattice, the lattice distortion occurred and leading to the shift of diffraction peaks. This also indicated the successful doping of Eu3+ ions into the YVO4 lattice.

The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra of YVO4:5 % Eu3+, YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2 and YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs were shown in Fig. 2(g-i). Fig. 2(g) indicated that the nanocomposites mainly consist of elements including Y, V, O, and Eu. The presence of Eu indicated successful doping of Eu3+ into YVO4. The detection of Cu signals was attributed to the copper grid used in TEM testing. Fig. 2(h) showed the characteristic peaks corresponding to Si elements mainly derived from the SiO2 shell, indicating successful encapsulation of SiO2. Additionally, it was evident that the introduction of carbon dots leads to a significant enhancement in the characteristic peak intensities of elements C and N, as shown in Fig. 2(i).

The overall X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) scanning spectrum of YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs was shown in Fig. 3(a). The peaks in the spectrum corresponded to the energy spectra of Eu 3d, V 2p, and Y 3d, located at 1135 eV, 517 eV, and 158 eV, respectively, which confirmed the successful doping of Eu3+ into YVO4. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s was shown in Fig. 3(b), which could be divided into four constrained energy peaks after fitting which located at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.7 eV, corresponding to sp2 (C-C or CC), C-O, C-N, and O-CO bonds, respectively. Fig. 3(c) displayed the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s, which mainly consisted of three peaks located at 529.8 eV, 531 eV, and 532 eV, corresponding to constrained binding energy of CO, Si-O, and C-O [21,22]. Fig. 3(d) showed the high-resolution spectrum of Si 2p, which could be divided into two binding energies, with 103 eV and 103.7 eV due to contributions from SiO2 and gel-like SiO2 [23,24]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s was shown in Fig. 3(e), indicating nitrogen was presented in three main forms, with peaks observed at 398.8 eV, 399.9 eV, and 401.4 eV, corresponding to amino-type nitrogen, pyrrole-type nitrogen, and graphite-type nitrogen respectively [25–27].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum in Fig. 4(a) displayed the surface functional groups of YVO4 before and after silica encapsulation, as well as those of YVO4@SiO2 after composite with CDs. For YVO4, the absorption band at 790 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of V-O [28]. After coating with silica, the appearance of absorption peaks at 1460 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 confirmed the presence of CO. The absorption peak of Si-OH at 810 cm−1 provided strong evidence for the successful encapsulation of silica. We could distinctly observe new characteristic peaks at 470 cm−1 which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the Si-O, indicating the forming of Si-O-Si bonds. The significant absorption peaks at 1325 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of the C-N and the N-H, which related to the amine functional groups C-N and N-H on the surface of YVO4. After compounding with CDs, the absorption of Si-OH decreased, while the absorption intensities near 1460 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 significantly increased due to the transformation from CO stretching vibration in COOH of CDs to CONH, indicating the successful bonding of CDs to the silica shell via amide bonds [29].

The Zeta potentials of YVO4:5 % Eu3+, YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2, and YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs were tested by a Zeta potential and laser particle size analyser. The results were shown in Fig. 4(b). It could be observed that the original potential of YVO4 was −25.3 mV. After surface modification with APTES, the introduction of positively charged -NH2 groups raised the potential from −25.3 mV to +32.6 mV. Finally, the potential of YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs decreased to +20.1 mV, which indicated that the negatively charged CDs successfully combined with YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2 through electrostatic forces.

The excitation spectra of prepared CDs under 530 nm monitoring and the emission spectra under 365 nm excitation were shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(a), there was a sharp absorption peak at 315 nm and a strong absorption peak at 365 nm. The peaks were typically attributed to π-π* transitions of CC and CN occurring in sp2 conjugation, as well as n-π* transitions of C-O and CO occurring in defect states [30,31]. Additionally, when excited at 365 nm, there was a prominent emission peak near 530 nm responding to the emission spectrum of CDs. By controlling the ratio of urea and citric acid, the emission colour of the CDs could be changed. The main reason might be that the higher molar ratio of urea to citric acid promoted the formation of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon bonds in the core of the CDs, extending the conjugation length to produce emission with longer wavelengths. Additionally, the formation of graphitic nitrogen structures reduced the bandgap, collectively resulting in a redshift in the emission of CDs. Consequently, as the molar ratio of urea to citric acid increased, the emission colour of the CDs could change from blue to green [31,32].

The excitation spectrum of YVO4: 5 % Eu3+ under 613 nm monitoring was shown in Fig. 5(c). The excitation spectrum was a broad band ranging from 225 nm to 350 nm with prominent peaks around 277 nm and 303 nm. The excitation peak at 277 nm was attributed to the absorption of VO4 3- clusters. The absorption of VO4 3- could be due to charge transfer from oxygen ligands (O2) to central vanadium atoms (V5+). In the crystal structure of YVO4, the original Td symmetry of VO4 3- clusters was lowered to D2d under crystal field effect, resulting in the splitting of degenerate energy levels of VO4 3- clusters [33]. The excitation peak at 303 nm raised from charge transfer (CT) transitions between Eu3+ and O2-, where electrons transferred from the O2- (2p6) orbitals to the empty 4 f6 orbitals of Eu3+ [13]. The weak excitation peak at 375 nm could be attributed to the transition 7F0→5L6 of Eu3+. The photoluminescence emission spectra of YVO4: X%Eu3+ (X=1, 3, 5, 8, 10) excited at 254 nm were shown in Fig. 5(d). The emission spectrum mainly consisted of peaks at 613 nm, 630 nm, and 640 nm, corresponding to transitions 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2 and 5D0→7F3 of Eu3+ ions, respectively [34–36]. As the concentration of Eu3+ increased, the emission intensity increased first and then decreased. When the Eu3+ doping concentration was 5 %, the emission intensity reached maximize. Further increased in the concentration of Eu3+ ions led to concentration quenching due to non-radiative energy transfer between neighbouring ions, resulting in a decrease in emission intensity.

The excitation and emission spectra of YVO4: 5 %Eu3+@SiO2@CDs were shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b) respectively. When monitored at 613 nm, the excitation spectrum exhibited a broad absorption peak ranging from 220 nm to 355 nm. While monitored at 455 nm, the excitation spectrum was composed of a broad absorption peak at 365 nm. Under excitation at 254 nm, the emission spectrum was mainly composed of transitions from the 5D0→7F1 (594 nm), 5D0→7F2 (613 nm) and 5D0→7F1 (630 nm) of Eu3+ ions. However, under excitation at 365 nm, a strong emission peak appeared at around 455 nm, which mainly attributed to the emission of CDs. This exhibits a noticeable blue shift compared to the emission peak of pure CDs at 530 nm. The luminescence mechanism of organosilane-functionalized CDs was not fully understood. Due to differences in the preparation materials and routes of CDs, not only the structure but also the form of the chemical bonds connecting with organosilanes might be different, resulting in changes in the emission wavelength [11]. Under excitation at 365 nm, YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs samples and pure CDs showed different fluorescence colours. This might be due to the influence of surface modification on CDs, resulting in a decrease in the content of CO and CN, and an increase in the optical gap. Additionally, the insets in Fig. 6(b) showed the solid-state luminescence of YVO4:5 % Eu3+@SiO2@CDs under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm, indicating that the composite material could achieve dual-mode solid-state luminescence.

Fig. 6(c-e) showed the photographs of the QR code anti-counterfeiting patterns under excitation at daylight, 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Under excitation at 254 nm, the QR code emitted red light, while under excitation at 365 nm, it appeared blue. The dual-mode luminescent anti-counterfeiting labels could provide broad prospects for applications in the field of information security.

In summary, we synthesized YVO4:Eu3+ nanocrystals by hydrothermal method and the optimal doping concentration of Eu3+ was determined to be 5 %. Subsequently, a layer of silica was successfully coated onto the YVO4:Eu3+ particles and introduced positively charged functional groups using APTES, providing effective conditions for the subsequent composite with CDs. Finally, the YVO4:5 %Eu3+@SiO2@CDs composite was prepared via electrostatic interactions. The luminescent colour of CDs could be tuned by controlling the proportions of the raw materials. When CDs was compounded with YVO4:Eu3+@SiO2 nanoparticles, the luminescent colour changed. This phenomenon might be attributed to the surface modification of CDs after composite formation, while leading to a decrease of the content of CO and CN. Finally, we utilized the prepared YVO4:5 %Eu3+@SiO2@CDs composite material to design a QR code pattern that emitted different colours under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively, demonstrating the significant potential in anti-counterfeiting applications. In addition, this composite material solved the problem of solid-state quenching of carbon dots, and could realize solid state luminescence, providing potential applications in lighting equipment, information protection and biosensors.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research data policy and data availability statements
ce:section.ce:para: The data are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Jiantao Lüa, associate professor, experimental operation and manuscript writing; Ting Fanb,c,d*, professor, experiment and thesis conception; Jie Liangb,c,d, Master student, experimental data analysis; Yayun Zhoua, Doctor, experimental data analysis; Tingting Denga, associate professor, Experimental guidance; Ruming Jiangb,c,d, Doctor, Resource provision.

Ruming Jiang: Resources. Tingting Deng: Validation. Yayun Zhou: Resources, Data curation. Jie Liang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ting Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiantao Lü: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Innovation team project of general colleges in Guangdong Province (2024KCXTD044), The key research projects of general colleges in Guangdong Province (2024ZDZX1043) and Young innovative talent project of general colleges in Guangdong Province (2024KQNCX149).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, M. Zheng, Y. Xiao, H. Dong, H. Zhang, J. Zhuang, H. Hu, B. Lei, Y. Liu, A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 312-318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid-state silicon nanoparticles with color-tunable photoluminescence and multifunctional applications
